author: @itllkeal

The cold air below the stove pipe will get heated from the stove and push it self out the top end of the pipe. Equals a redneck heat pump.

author: @Afro408
text: When we were kids, in the early 50’s, dad used to put some house bricks in our slow combustion stove after dinner was cooked and when the bricks were hot, would wrap them in pieces of blanket. These hot brick parcels were put between the sheets at the foot of our beds and we’d be warm all night in winter. Better than hot water bottles and no wet accidents. 😁👍

Could also simply build a window insert with a metal flue pipe going through it to exhaust the rocket stove to the outside for charging up the sand and then once the sand is charged up and the fire is out, the window insert could be popped out and set next to the rocket stove and the window be closed.

author: @MrGammyballz
text: The rocket stove I bought has a flue coming out the back made from 3inch exhaust pipe so I can use it indoors. The flue goes though a small hole in kitchen wall then runs up external wall(about 2m high). It would be fairly simple to modify rocket stoves without a flue to receive one.

author: @ClownWhisper
text: If you make a real rocket mass heater the way it was intended to be made you can have a very very small pipe going out outside to take a very tiny bit if any smoke out. I would suggest that everybody watch rocket mass heaters and watch a build as far as the old school method of heating your house where a chamber is used to reburn and reburn and reburn the emissions from the burning wood to get ideas on how to almost completely eliminate any emissions from the burn. People have taken this concept and bastardized it down to these little metal burning mechanisms that are not really rocket mass stoves realize the middle word mass it's meant to heat a large mass of rock clay and sand in order to heat your home. These were never meant to be small metal burners for the sake of burning a little bit of wood and heating up a pot of water. Rednecks in the United states started doing that and I don't even know why. Look on YouTube for the original videos on how to build a rocket mass heater for your home and you'll see what it was originally intended for

author: @mike29412
text:
Your power videos are brilliant.   But I'd like to see a video for some kind of solar heater for recycling plastic.   As I understand it the common shopping bag is very low melt point and a a good(ish) insulator.  So cast the molten plastic into "bricks and use them inbetween brick courses (safe from the UV) or perhaps slugs that could be dropped inside concrete blocks...  Or made into sheets to work as vapor and thermal barriers?

If you make a million dollars & win prizes I just want a nice mention at the Nobel prize ceremony and a beer...

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: I wonder how hard it would be to build a second layer like a sheath that has air gap between it and the stove outside, and seal it so you can pipe air out of the house and into the cavity then pull it back in through another pipe after it is hot, all with out having to have any of the smoke and debris from the fire contaminating the air on it's way in. (and run this through say a small board that a window can be propped open keeping the cold air from outside from coming in, but allowing air to be sucked into the outgoing pipe and another port for the incoming heated air back into the window. (I do something like this for my cat where I hang a flap over 1/4 of the window and the other 3/4 of the window opening, is blocked with a board and some insulation so she can push the little flap open to get in and out, but only a strong gust of wind can really push that flap open much at all, otherwise it remains closed the rest of the time avoiding air flow when the cat is not going or coming, or coming and going. lol) )

replies.author: @judyofthewoods

author: @PaulA-ed8xs
text: There was a video on YouTube I saw a while back where a guy made a Swedish log torch and ran a bit of bent steel pipe straight up the middle of it. As the air in the pipe was heated it drew in cold air at the bottom and pushed hot air out the top of the pipe which he poked into a tent to keep it warm in snowy conditions. Would be easy enough to weld a pipe to the back of the rocket stove and do the same thing.

author: @J8n3eyr3
text:
Further down in the comments a chimney sand battery ring is discussed by Richard Cranium and another.
I could see this being made from recycled cookie tins. METHOD: cut the chimney-sized holes in the top and bottom, weld in the center bit of metal in the bottom, fill with sand, weld the center and lid together and around the outside, and slide that down over the chimney. You can stack it all back up inside or put them in the little stoves or foot warmers. 
I think it would be cheap enough to allowing making a second set to have heating before the first loses all warmth.

author: @wendlo1
text: for cows.. and deer etc.. they make salt blocks.. about ten pounds or dollars.. give or take.. why not use salt blocks to store energy.. put handles on both sides and drill a hole in the middle of the salt block.. the salt storage will hold the heat a long time..  place two of them on the chimney.. the heat in the rocket stove isn't enough to melt the salt but will store the energy to be used in the house.. just lift it off and take it in.. you can place a magic heat heat reclaimer on top to blow the heat around or simply a fan..

author: @jaagee6980

author: @timoblake5689

Last week after a good cleaning I grabbed some slate, soapstone or some such stome from an old woodstove layed them on the bottom. Did a burn for about two hours on Christmas Day and at 7ish that same day the firebox was at 94 degrees Fahrenheit some 4 hrs later. Best thing I have done to my house.

author: @elainepanthera5444
text: You have been reading my mind...

author: @regwatson2017
text:
Sorry to be negative Rob - technically yes, you've brought the heat inside. But how practical day-to-day is that really for say elderly,  disabled or time-poor amongst us who can't just go outside to start and maintain a fire in a rocket stove, then carry great lumps of stone in and out of the house and squeezing a potentially second-degree causing burning stone into a small box ? And even with a larger piece of stone (assuming you could somehow suspend it over the rocket stove chimney safely) and then manoeuvre it indoors, you aren't going to be able to effectively radiate heat to warm anything but a smallish area of the house in the middle of winter. What you've shown, fair enough, may be a sort of solution for some but it is really only good for warming one person's feet and that's about it.

I know you love rocket stoves and yes they are great to enjoy cooking your breakfast bacon and eggs with your Son outside a workshop on a cold day but I still believe that rocket stoves aren't a really practical form of heating in suburban living. Even if you bring them inside and go to the expense of installing a chimney for them you still can't make very effective use of the high speed burn with most of the heat going up the chimney. Then you've got to have a big inconvenient supply of combustible fuel and also spend loads of time constantly stoking them to keep them from going out because of the speed and intensity of the burn. They are great as a conversation piece around a camping fire or as a camp cooker - the noise they make, the quick heat up and the need for manual intervention constantly having to throw wood on them every few minutes which will keep the closet pyromaniacs happy but so far I am yet to see them as practical indoor heating solution.

Loved the idea of the Vevor diesel heater running on waste cooking oil and a couple of additions. Now you're talking !

author: @davemillan3360
text: Would it be possible to extract the fumes of the rocket stove with a sort of compressor pump and pump them through a water bath to remove all the sooth and then just have carbon monoxide left to expel through ventilating the home? The water bath should probably have a bit of detergent in it to break surface tension / bonds of the water and you would need to pump the extracted fumes through some sort of fine mesh to create tiny bubbles (a bit like an aquarium) and then maybe agitate the water to mix the air bubbles and the water. If you run that compressor pump with a steam engine powered by the rocket stove you would come full circle. Getting rid of carbon monoxide through ventilation is easy enough, but it's the sooth and smoke that is the problem for indoor fires. The water would get murky but you could have it sit for a while to have all the sooth settle in the bottom and then recover the water and get rid of the sooth.

author: @salaciouscreations4323
text:
I favour sand battery for heat storage.   I placed hollow copper pipe inside the 3 Tim can tall units and filled with sand around them.  Added a 90° elbow and ran 6" pipe into the methanol flame.    Having the tubes hollow does make the bottom of the batteries warmer than a sand filled pipe.    Gives me about 2 extra hours of heat from a methanol burn which lasts an hour at 500ml.    I have no idea how people are getting 5 hour burns from methanol so that as my solution. Store all the heat I can in bricks pavers and sandbatterys.    We haven't been cold this winter even when it was -9°c.   Pavers make great tops to store heat and are cheap.  It's not all house heating but having a warm room makes all the difference between living in the cold.  

Can't do much about electric use but hopefully the lack of the heating bill offsets the winter bill once we are over the cold months.    The gas is just used for hotwater now.

author: @LOFIGSD
text: Got to think about weight and transport, how much heat stored in that mass and how much it gives off for a period of time.  You ideally want a fluid that can be pumped to where it is needed, using a mechanical solution, rather than a human lifting a solid mass, so I would suggest looking at repurposing an oil filled radiator, pump heated oil from your rocket stove inside via a pipe,  use the hot air from the rocket stove to drive the pump.

author: @cavelvlan25
text: 7 inch tube J style stove with ash catch.. long wood closable vertical feed. Cast grates in burn chamber. 7x28" heat riser with 8x 28"  insulating tube surrounding filled with diy firebrick 16x42inch dome capped tube encasing it down to ashtray.  With exhaust coming out the middle back running up. Has 1 inch fresh air feed to post burn and small gap to burn chamber. heat exchange is 16x7 inch tube with flat top. split in half 4 holes with half-pipe wrapping down and up each side of stove. The then whole thing in a cut up 55 drum backfilled with firebrick.

author: @RG-cf6dn
text:
I know someone rather "woodsie" who made a rocket stove out of a horizontal compressor air tank.  He cut 6 inch aluminum duct pipe match the circumference of the tank drilled some holes in it right above the compressor tank to draw in cool air.  Then he uses regular old flex pipe attached to said ducting bringing the heat in through a window.  Uses a little window frame built of plywood he pipes it through w/ little fan suck the heat up.  Where the flex pipe meets the wood frame he used another little piece of 6 inch duct pipe creating an insulation layer where he made fashioned his own heat refractory material out of plaster and sand which he found via a video here at youTube.

Last I spoke to him he told me he was going to modify it by putting a vent in the heat exchange duct pipe to mix more air upwind of the convection holes so he could perhaps better adjust temperature as he said it puts out too much heat.  I believe he said he heated 800 SQ Ft thereabouts with it.

Whole things on wheels, comes apart easy, no modifications needed to his place, takes about two minutes to setup.

author: @justinw1765
text:
Why couldn't you put a flue pipe on it and then put a barrel over it with a hole for the flue pipe to go through, and then another hole to vent the hot air into a window via an insulated window insert?  For the window insert, you can use plywood treated with high heat silicone and then put fumed silica in between the panels.  

   For best results, you would want to insulate the barrel that collects the hot air and put a little, very breathable insulation at the top of the flue pipe.

author: @Reman1975
text:
A mate of mine in Sweden built himself a large steel framed workshop next to his bungalow. Inside the workshop he's got a bloody great log burning stove that he made to take pretty long lumps of log (There's less chainsawing and less time lost to go tend to the fire if it can easily take 3'6" long logs !). The top of that burner is a long flat plate, and during winter, on top of that goes 3 ruddy great lumps of stainless steel. Each of these lumps are about a foot long and 18ish inch in diameter, so he has to use an engine hoist on eye bolts to move them. They were just offcuts he had kicking around from a big turning job, but each one of these "Offcuts" must be 2 to 300kg !

Throughout the day these things get hotter and hotter. Then in the evening, before shutting everything off and tidying up, he loads these onto a stout trolley he made for the job, and wheels them into his house. He then leaves them to do their thing while he finishes up in the workshop. By the time he gets back to the house it's warm, and he says that these things save him from having to light a fire in his home through all but the harshest of winters.


I remember once wondering what kind of paraffin or diesel heater he'd need to use get an equivalent BTU per hour that these lumps of stainless put out, but there's just too many variables to even start trying to do the maths. I don't even know what temperature they get up to, or how constant the final temperature is from day to day....... But I'm still curious, because if it's enough to fend off a Swedish winter, the output must be pretty high.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Same idea to the next level. Use a bucket of stones with a bunch of smaller holes on the bottom and stacked at the top of the chimney. The hot exhaust then rises through the rock 
 
If there were smoke left in the air spaces between the rocks, then  convection will then pull it out just before bringing it inside.

replies.author: @judyofthewoods
replies.text:
Anything that is heated over an open flame is likely going to small sooty. With this idea you can use a smaller bucket on the stove and then transfer it into a larger bucket with lid to seal in the smell. Could also be more decorative. Maybe a mini glazed no-chimney "Kachelofen". Have two of the smaller ones to swap them out. The outer bucket will at first be a little slower releasing the heat from within, but evens it out a bit over time.
Alternatively, use an intermediate hot plate so the soot stays with the stove.

author: @fenceup07944931177
text:
I think the ideal solution would be a sand battery to absorb as much heat as possible from a shot burn say 2hr or so. 
Then I'm thinking if that heat could preheat the return to a conventional central heating boiler. The boiler wouldn't have to work so hard, but could do all the work of controlling the circulation, of the heat and regulating the temperature. 
To keep it simple, if the rocket stove was its own system, and some form of heat exchanger between it and the central heating, would mitigate some problems and risk.

author: @corinneyeager
text:
In Korea (n/s ?) they build a wood burning area below grade running the heat under the house in channels... But how to retro fit that? Mobile homes maybe fins in a container outside with only heat exits to under... No smoke warm floor....
My brains running to many scenarios....
TO THE SKETCHPAD...........

author: @jerrodlopes186
text:
I've got similar ideas. I've done this with a small sand battery that I then put into a ceramic chimenea in the house. 

What I really want to do is put a pellet hopper on my rocket stove so that it'll burn for 8 hours nonstop. Then just run a heat exchanger/radiator from the stovepipe through a thimble installed in a window. I found a little motorcycle radiator on Amazon for less than $20. That'll be a good start.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Personally, I don't want to have to be running in and out in rotten weather to keep swapping out items like that.

And if one were crippled, it would be pretty much unattainable.

The copper coil is something that one need not spend all their time tending to, be they in hale health or on the edge of death.

Admittedly, if one is on such bad health, its likely they will be relying on somebody else to be building the stove, as well as tending any heavy pieces of thermal mass.

Me personally, I'm just fine with the stove outside of the house, but I'm going to stick with the radiator and heat exchanger option.

Again, saying something that annoys some people, but if it is built CORRECTLY...

"Its more 'efficient' "

author: @anthonymellor174
text: Could you pass the flu through Gideons packed with stones for underfloor heating send it round in a loop an outside chimney

author: @Xanderbelle
text: Cooking facilities as well. A plate on top of the fire box for a pot or kettle an extra box on the side as an oven then a tray underneath as a grill.

author: @Youtoobe0Guy0Awesome
text: Removable sand battery?

author: @malHHkenny
text:
I'm experimenting with running a plain steel tube at a rising angle over the flame. The low end of the tube is about 1 foot from the top of the stove. After the hot spot, about 4 feet of the tube rise and enter the tent or small camper. The heat rising in the tube draws cold fresh air. I've managed to get 68° fahrenheit (20° C) up from 40° (5° C) into the space.

That part's no problem.

But the stove I'm using is not burning oil cleanly. (I have almost unlimited access to fresh vegetable oil.) Smoke could get into the cool end of the steel tube and smoke means fuel wasted and low flame temperature.

If I do manage a tall enough chimney section to get a good "rocket" burn, that would mean that the end of the tube that needs to be low, is forced to be high.

(The fresh air tube could run through the center height of the chimney. But I'd only want to build that setup if/when there's a satisfactorily clean burn anyway.)

replies.author: @jerrodlopes186
replies.text: Just mix a small percentage of paraffin or kerosene into the oil. It'll burn cleaner at relatively small expense. Good idea you have there.

replies.author: @malHHkenny
replies.text: @jerrodlopes186 I can't deny that this is worth a try.

author: @steveciarico9824
text: What if you had a large carousel halfway between the rocket stove outside and the house inside. The "hot plate"  would be round and on a vertical axle that spins as fast as a rotisserie moving heat from the stove through an insulated horizontal slit in the house. Ok...maybe that's a long shot...lol

author: @Route72Trucks
text:
Another fella on YouTube (can’t remember name) built a large sand battery around the chimney.  After an hour of warming the battery he would split  the two taking the large battery  (80 kilos) inside and leaving the stove out doors.    

He reported that it worked great and keep his living room  room too warm at times.  Stays warm through the night.

replies.author: @RichardCranium321
replies.text: I saw that one too.  His design was well thought out but a little bit clunky.   Although i guess u can't have thermal mass without the "mass"

replies.author: @Route72Trucks
replies.text: @RichardCranium321  seems you could build something into a cart of sorts.  Easily wheeled around. Feet that won’t allow it to be toppled.  Cool idea for sure.

replies.author: @RichardCranium321
replies.text: @Route72Trucks I was thinking more like a set of 4 or 5 stackable rings and a central spindle.  The spindle itself acts as an alignment for the transport cart (and extra thermal mass) but it's hole is also the "chimney" slot for the stove.  Imagine a flapjack stack with a vertical hole down through the middle. You can move each pancake individually yet still make up the same size stack and it eats just the same.

replies.author: @GadgetViper
replies.text: @RichardCranium321 this makes perfect sense

replies.author: @RichardCranium321
replies.text: @GadgetViper did it??  it made perfect sense in my head but after reading it, it sounds more confusing in text

author: @pattayaguideorg
text: The Force was weak in this video and so was the idea lol. Had a cast iron wood burner inside my house, was the complete opposite to a rocket stove, burned slowly all night or day with minimal local wood, up the flute and out the chimney, of all the ways I've tried to keep warm over the years, hands down the best and most enjoyable and extremely cheap, free wood cut on the side off the road, kept the house lovely and warm all winter, no dicking around. make an exhaust exit, done.

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: in many areas of the UK you are not allowed to emit smoke, smokeless fuels must be used (or a rocket stove that emits no smoke), so often other solutions must be found, these are just ideas that can be adapted or copied as a way of getting the heat indoors where you cant run alternative or want to avoid more expensive options.

replies.author: @jerrodlopes186
replies.text: @grendel1960a even wood can be smokeless if seasoned and burned properly. You all give up too easily on that side of the pond. Pellets can, and usually do, burn very cleanly. I have a small rocket stove for which there is a pellet hopper available. If I owned my home I would install it in a heartbeat and never be cold again.

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: @jerrodlopes186 many years back now (in another house that was a listed property (17th century -limited changes allowed to the exterior) we used to burn wood in the standard fireplace of the house, the wood was bought as a load of slabs or offcuts / trimmings from the local sawmill at £5 a load, a load would completely fill the car (you had to collect) but would keep the house warm for several months, I also used to stop and pick up fallen wood b the roadside on the way home - fortunately at the time we lived in a small village with no smoke free zones, now I live in a city centre, and the smoke free zone starts 20 foot from my back garden wall, so I have to be careful, also since the house used to have forced air gas heating (and now has gas central heating) there is no chimney built in, so alternate methods are more appropriate. the cost of a woodburner installation is even more exorbitant than running gas or electric heating, and is not something that we can DIY.

replies.author: @jerrodlopes186
replies.text: @grendel1960a your government has you all boxed up nice and neat, haven't they? It's headed that direction very quickly here, too. I live in California. It's very obvious, here at least, that these regulations have little to do with the environment and a lot to do with certain people making money. Just before reading your reply I made a couple of DIY oil lamps and put them in my ceramic fireplace. They're happily burning away and giving off some heat with a little help from a strategically placed computer fan. I feel badly for you over there, and anyone under such harsh and heavy-handed rule. Good luck to you.

replies.author: @pattayaguideorg
replies.text: @grendel1960a ok, thanks for the info, cheers.

author: @KayWessel
text: I think heating air and moving it using 2 air vents in the wall with natural flow of air would be a simple solution. The air should be heated around the base and the chimney before raising up and into the house. No maintanance, no water which can freeze and no fans. Just a hole 30 cm from the floor and another hole 30 cm from the ceiling.The heat should move the air from bottom to top without any fan.

replies.author: @401ksolar
replies.text: I have a south facing solar heat panel which uses convection in the same way that you are describing, I do have a fan hooked to a 5 watt solar panel that blows heat from that room into the house at the top of the door, that convection certainly does work though.

replies.author: @kevinleebailey
replies.text: A stove top fan addapted to fit inside any ducting would definitely move more warm ( hot ) air. This could push warmed air out of the bottom vent and pull cooler air from the top vent making this system more effective at warming the same space.

author: @grendel1960a
text: I have a little woodburner (much like your vevor stove) and recently i had a washing machine fail, now I use one of the shaped lumps of concrete that were added round the drum, I put it on the hot plate of the stove, run the stove for about an hour (3 dried logs worth) , and the concrete lump absorbs the heat as it comes off, I have one of those peltier fans and that carries on running (over 50 degrees) for well over an hour after the stove has stopped burning, I can come back several hours later and still that lump is warm

replies.author: @tesla747
replies.text: Ah, you basically built a mass heater. Rocket mass heaters are big in the earth ships and the off-grid homes.

author: @GREATLORDPOOH
text: Put a coil of copper around the chimney and run it inside with another coil/heat exchanger with a fan do it right and you wouldn't have to have a pump

author: @juanpabloabalde
text: Cheers from Uruguay!! Love the way you think! Simple is always better!  Something Day im going to show you a generator that im making for my acuaponics sistem. Have a great New year!!

author: @samuelthomasengardio
text: My dad installed an indoor wood burning unit called a Caddy in his basement, he used it for many years while working locally. Typically, he'd spend less than $300 in wood cost annually to heat his house in Michigan. If you don't mind splitting wood and have the time to deal with the matter, it isn't a bad way to go.

replies.author: @samuelthomasengardio
replies.text: @SheriffofYouTube It's work. If you want to run out with a saw and collect fallen trees, split the wood, you'll save alot of money. But that is time and it depends on what your time is worth to you.

replies.author: @russellzauner
replies.text:
@SheriffofYouTube Woodcutting permits here vary by county but averages $18 USD for 2-4 cords of wood; you can harvest anything down within 100ft of access roads and of course you're allowed to clear established service roads if they're blocked, which can yield logs of alder, fir, spruce, and several other species that are large enough to slab out.  
You can get a cord of mixed hardwood around the state for $150 if you don't look for it literally during the winter (which a lot of people here do because it's available they just double the prices during the cold so there's basically a lazy tax on it), but again, the permits here are issued by total quantity of wood not time limited or number of trips so you could go out and fill the trunk of your compact car 30x if you want so not having a truck isn't an excuse, really.  We end up leaving more wood than we came with every time we go camping lol

replies.author: @DetroitFettyghost
replies.text: Fellow Michigander here

Unfortunately this is the way of the WEF MACHINE now .. 
But that's a great idea if living elsewhere less restrictive and plenty of woodlands
